docs.title: Karva (0.1.4)
docs.text: A Python test framework, written in Rust.

docs.title: karva
docs.text:
A Python test runner.

Usage 
karva <COMMAND>
Commands karva test

Run tests

karva version

Display Karva's version

karva help

Print this message or the help of the given subcommand(s)

docs.title: karva test
docs.text:
Run tests

Usage 
karva test [OPTIONS] [PATH]...
Arguments PATHS

List of files, directories, or test functions to test [default: the project root]

Options --help, -h

Print help

--show-output, -s

Show Python stdout during test execution

--test-prefix test-prefix

The prefix of the test functions

[default: test]

--verbose, -v

Use verbose output (or -vv and -vvv for more verbose output)

docs.title: karva version
docs.text:
Display Karva's version

Usage 
karva version
Options --help, -h

Print help

docs.title: karva help
docs.text:
Print this message or the help of the given subcommand(s)

Usage 
karva help [COMMAND]

docs.title: Constraints
docs.text: We only discover package level fixtures from a conftest.py file in the root of that package.

docs.title: Fixture Types
docs.text: Karva supports different types of fixtures based on their scope:

docs.title: Function Scope
docs.text: The default scope. The fixture is created for each test function.

docs.title: Module Scope
docs.text: The fixture is created once per test module.

docs.title: Package Scope
docs.text:
The fixture is created once per test package.

This means that every package that is a sub-package of the package with the fixture will create the fixture if and only if it has a module that requires the fixture.

For example, if we have the following structure:

calculator
├── src
└── tests
    ├── conftest.py
    ├── bar
    │   └── test_bar.py
    └── foo
        └── test_foo.py

If we have the following fixture in tests/conftest.py:

@fixture(scope="package")
def package_fixture():
    return "package"

And if the fixtures is used in tests/foo/test_foo.py and tests/bar/test_bar.py then fixture will be created once for the foo package and once for the bar package.

If you wanted it to only create the fixture once you should use the session scope.

docs.title: Session Scope
docs.text: The fixture is created once per test session.

docs.title: Dependent fixtures
docs.text:
We support fixtures that depend on other fixtures.

from karva import fixture

@fixture
def function_fixture():
    return "function"

@fixture
def dependent_fixture(function_fixture: str) -> str:
    return function_fixture + "dependent"

def test_dependent(dependent_fixture: str):
    assert dependent_fixture == "functiondependent"

docs.title: Finalizers
docs.text:
We support finalizers. These are called after the scope of the fixture has finished running.

from karva import fixture

@fixture
def finalizer_fixture():
    print("Finalizer fixture initialized")
    yield 1
    print("Finalizer fixture finalizer called")

def test_finalizer(finalizer_fixture: int):
    assert finalizer_fixture == 1

docs.title: Auto-use fixtures
docs.text:
We support auto-use fixtures. These are fixtures that are automatically used in their scope.

from karva import fixture

data = {}

@fixture(auto_use=True)
def add_data():
    data.update(value=True)

def test_value():
    assert data.get('value')

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
uv init --lib calculator

This will give us a project that looks like this:

calculator
├── pyproject.toml
├── README.md
└── src
    └── calculator
        ├── __init__.py
        └── py.typed

We can then create our core logic in src/calculator/__init__.py.

class Calculator:
    def add(self, a: int, b: int) -> int:
        return a + b

We can then create our fixtures in tests/conftest.py.

from karva import fixture

@fixture
def calculator() -> Calculator:
    return Calculator()

We can then create our tests in tests/test_add.py.

from calculator import Calculator

def test_add(calculator: Calculator):
    assert calculator.add(1, 2) == 3

Then, we'll add karva to our project.

uv add --dev karva

We can then run our tests with uv run karva test.

uv run karva test

This will give us a report of our tests.

All checks passed!

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
Karva is available as karva on PyPI.

Use karva directly with uvx:

uvx karva test
uvx karva version

Or install karva with uv, or pip:

# With uv.
uv tool install karva@latest

# Add karva to your project.
uv add --dev karva

# With pip.
pip install karva

Once installed, you can use karva to run your tests:

karva test

Or to get the version of karva you're using:

karva version

docs.title: Tutorial
docs.text: This tutorial will walk you through the basics of using Karva.

docs.title: Getting started
docs.text:
We will first create a new project using uv.

uv init --lib calculator

This will give us a project that looks like this:

calculator
├── pyproject.toml
├── README.md
└── src
    └── calculator
        ├── __init__.py
        └── py.typed

We can then create our core logic in src/calculator/__init__.py.

class Calculator:
    def add(self, a: int, b: int) -> int:
        return a + b

We can then create our tests in tests/test_add.py.

from calculator import Calculator

def test_add():
    calculator = Calculator()
    assert calculator.add(1, 2) == 3

Then, we'll add karva to our project.

uv add --dev karva

We can then run our tests with uv run karva test.

uv run karva test

This will give us a report of our tests.

All checks passed!

docs.title: Parametrize
docs.text:
This works like pytest's parametrize decorator.

Note

When mixing fixtures and parametrize, the parametrize values take priority.

First, here is a small example:

import karva

@karva.tags.parametrize("a", [1, 2, 3])
def test_function(a: int):
    assert a > 0

Then running uv run karva test -v will provide the following output:

INFO Discovering tests...
INFO Discovered 1 test in 1 file
INFO running  | test_parametrize::test_function [1]
INFO passed   | test_parametrize::test_function [1]
INFO running  | test_parametrize::test_function [2]
INFO passed   | test_parametrize::test_function [2]
INFO running  | test_parametrize::test_function [3]
INFO passed   | test_parametrize::test_function [3]
Passed tests: 3
All checks passed!

We can also parametrize multiple arguments:

import karva

@karva.tags.parametrize(("a", "b"), [(1, 4), (2, 5), (3, 6)])
def test_function(a: int, b: int):
    assert a > 0 and b > 0

Then running uv run karva test -v will provide the following output:

INFO Discovering tests...
INFO Discovered 1 test in 1 file
INFO running  | test_parametrize::test_function [1, 4]
INFO passed   | test_parametrize::test_function [1, 4]
INFO running  | test_parametrize::test_function [2, 5]
INFO passed   | test_parametrize::test_function [2, 5]
INFO running  | test_parametrize::test_function [3, 6]
INFO passed   | test_parametrize::test_function [3, 6]
Passed tests: 3
All checks passed!

We can also mix fixtures and parametrize:

import karva

@karva.fixture
def b() -> int:
    return 1

@karva.tags.parametrize("a", [1, 2])
def test_function(a: int, b: int):
    assert a > 0 and b > 0

Then running uv run karva test -v will provide the following output:

INFO Discovering tests...
INFO Discovered 1 test in 1 file
INFO running  | test_parametrize::test_function [1, 1]
INFO passed   | test_parametrize::test_function [1, 1]
INFO running  | test_parametrize::test_function [2, 1]
INFO passed   | test_parametrize::test_function [2, 1]
Passed tests: 2
All checks passed!

We can also use multiple decorators:

This will result in a sort of cartesian product of the parametrize values.

import karva

@karva.tags.parametrize("a", [1, 2])
@karva.tags.parametrize("b", [1, 2])
def test_function(a: int, b: int):
    assert a > 0 and b > 0

Then running uv run karva test -v will provide the following output:

INFO Discovering tests...
INFO Discovered 1 test in 1 file
INFO running  | test_parametrize::test_function [1, 1]
INFO passed   | test_parametrize::test_function [1, 1]
INFO running  | test_parametrize::test_function [2, 1]
INFO passed   | test_parametrize::test_function [2, 1]
INFO running  | test_parametrize::test_function [1, 2]
INFO passed   | test_parametrize::test_function [1, 2]
INFO running  | test_parametrize::test_function [2, 2]
INFO passed   | test_parametrize::test_function [2, 2]
Passed tests: 4
All checks passed!

We can also still use pytest's parametrize decorator:

import pytest

@pytest.mark.parametrize("a", [1, 2])
def test_function(a: int):
    assert a > 0

Then running uv run karva test -v will provide the following output:

INFO Discovering tests...
INFO Discovered 1 test in 1 file
INFO running  | test_parametrize::test_function [1]
INFO passed   | test_parametrize::test_function [1]
INFO running  | test_parametrize::test_function [2]
INFO passed   | test_parametrize::test_function [2]
Passed tests: 2
All checks passed!
